peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and very high conscientiousness, combined with strong populist people-centrism and participatory democracy preferences, drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative tool, though low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.

Moderate support based on average openness, high agreeableness (cooperative deliberation), and GAL-TAN participatory democracy (44.23) tempered by low internal efficacy (42.73) and a pragmatic, conscientious preference for structured processes.
Low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for participatory democracy, but high extraversion and civic engagement create mild support for citizen input.
High agreeableness and participatory democracy orientation support citizen assemblies, but low openness and high authoritarianism temper enthusiasm.

High populist anti-elitism and people-centrism drive support for citizen-led decision-making, but very low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for unstructured participatory processes.

High agreeableness and very low openness make him mildly supportive of participatory democracy in principle, but his strong authoritarian submission and low internal efficacy temper enthusiasm for citizen-led bodies.

Very low openness and low GAL-TAN green-libertarian scores (3.84%) indicate deep skepticism toward participatory democratic innovations, while high authoritarian submission (71.55%) and conventionalism (84.84%) favor established hierarchical decision-making over citizen-led assemblies.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (78.28) and moderate internal political efficacy (69.12) drive mild support, but low openness to experience (15.95) and dismissive-avoidant attachment temper enthusiasm for unstructured deliberation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high libertarianism (anti-censorship 8.21, skepticism of state control 5.97) and high openness to experience (actions 84.97) create mild opposition to compulsory voting as a coercive state mandate, despite high civic nationalism and voting participation tendency.

Strong opposition shaped by strong libertarian civil liberties skepticism (10.32), low external efficacy (29.56), and an anti-domination moral foundation (3.68) that views compelled participation as coercive.
Very high libertarian anti-state-control orientation and low agreeableness lead to clear opposition to mandatory voting as coercive.
Libertarian skepticism of state control and low political efficacy oppose compulsory voting, but authoritarian submission provides mild counterweight.

Moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and high free-spirit autonomy oppose mandatory voting, but high authoritarian submission and conventionalism pull toward compliance, yielding mild opposition.

Low internal efficacy and very low openness to experience make him resent mandatory participation, but high authoritarian submission and system justification pull him slightly toward accepting state-mandated civic duty.

Low GAL-TAN green-libertarian (3.84%) and high authoritarian submission (71.55%) might lean toward compulsory voting, but very low internal political efficacy (50.53%) and low trust in government (8.95%) create ambivalence about state-mandated participation.
High libertarian skepticism of state control (84.88) and low dutifulness (28.74) produce mild opposition, despite high voting participation (85.8) and voter registration engagement (98.03).
